source.title: Cumulative use of strong anticholinergics and incident dementia: a prospective cohort study.

confidence_rationale: Strong prospective cohort (ACT, n=3434, 7.3y mean follow-up, computerised dispensing data so dose is well-measured) with a clear monotonic dose-response between cumulative anticholinergic TSDDs and incident dementia. Directly anchors the cumulative-dose limb of Q-031, but the design cannot dissociate REM-mediated from diurnal-cholinergic-deficit pathways; that limit pulls the score below 0.85.
failure_signatures: Cumulative dose alone is a coarse exposure marker -- it conflates daytime cholinergic blockade (relevant to Pathway 2, MECH-175) with sleep-period blockade (relevant to Pathway 1 / MECH-173 nocturnal REM suppression). The study cannot tell the two apart.
TSDDs are calibrated to a generic standardised daily dose, not to plasma half-life or CNS penetration, so two equally-dosed drugs with very different sleep-period CNS exposure are pooled.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Higher cumulative anticholinergic exposure (measured as 10-year TSDDs of strong anticholinergics) is associated with a dose-graded increase in risk of incident dementia and Alzheimer disease in a prospective cohort of community-dwelling adults age 65+.
mapping.ree_translation: Q-031 limb (b): supports the hypothesis that cumulative anticholinergic dose is itself a dementia risk factor over decade timescales. The study establishes a dose-response curve but does not partition that signal into the two REE-relevant pathways: (i) MECH-173 nocturnal REM suppression accumulating REM-debt years across decades, vs (ii) MECH-175 diurnal cholinergic deficit accumulating daytime sensorium-loop noise. Both pathways predict a monotonic dose-response, so this paper is consistent with either or both.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The ACT cohort had no PSG sleep architecture data, so REM% was not measured as a mediator. This means the paper cannot test the central mediation question Q-031 raises. It is best read as 'cumulative anticholinergic burden is a real dementia risk factor' and not as 'cumulative anticholinergic burden acts via REM suppression specifically'.
mapping.source_context: Population-based prospective cohort, Group Health (Seattle), age 65+, computerised pharmacy dispensing data, mean 7.3y follow-up, Cox proportional hazards adjusted for demographics, behaviours, and comorbidities.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality is high (large prospective cohort, modern pharmacy dispensing, well-adjusted Cox model). Mapping fidelity is moderate because the paper measures the cumulative-dose limb of Q-031 but cannot dissociate the two REE-postulated pathways. Transfer risk is low (older adults, real prescriptions, real dementia outcomes -- the same population Q-031 is about).
